You open the fridge. Stare for 45 seconds. Close it. Open it again. Nothing changed. Order DoorDash.

This is not a food problem. It's a decision problem. And nobody's solving it right.

I had half a butternut squash, a can of chickpeas, and wilting spinach.

Typed it into PantryMate. Got a curry recipe in 30 seconds. Made it. Ate it. Would make again.

That's the whole pitch. pantrymate.net

The most underrated cooking skill isn't technique. It's knowing what to make with what you already have.

Most of us stare into a full fridge and see nothing. That gap between "I have food" and "I know what to make" is costing you time, money, and dinner peace.

PantryMate closes that gap. Type your ingredients, get dinner ideas in 30 seconds. No account needed to start.

Link in bio → pantrymate.net

Real question for you: when you say "there's nothing to eat" — is the fridge actually empty, or are you just blanking on what to make?

Be honest. I'll go first: my fridge is usually pretty full and I still order takeout twice a week.

The problem isn't ingredients. It's the decision. Drop your answer below 👇

I built a startup from Namibia. Current MRR: embarrassingly small. But growing.

The product is PantryMate — you type what's in your fridge, AI tells you what to cook. Simple problem. 30 seconds to solve.

I built it because I kept staring into my fridge and closing it without a plan. Turns out: a lot of people do the same thing.

If you've ever shipped something with ugly early numbers and kept going — I'd love to know how you thought about it.

→ pantrymate.net

Unpopular opinion: meal planning apps are solving the wrong problem.

They make you plan a week ahead. But most people don't know what they want for dinner at 5pm, let alone next Thursday.

The real problem is decision fatigue at the moment you need to cook. That's what PantryMate solves.

Type what you have → get dinner now. No planning required.

I used to throw out $30–$40 of food a week. Not because I didn't care — just because by the time I figured out what to cook, I'd already ordered something.

Started doing this instead: before I grocery shop, I type everything I still have into PantryMate (pantrymate.net). It tells me what I can actually cook with what's already there. Usually I get 2–3 more dinners out of stuff I would've tossed.

Last week it turned: half a butternut squash, a can of chickpeas, and some wilting spinach into a curry I'd make again.

Anyone else doing something similar to cut food waste? What's your method?

What's the most random thing you've cooked because you refused to go to the grocery store?

I'll start: tuna + leftover rice + whatever that sauce was. Ate it. Zero regrets.

Drop yours below — this is a judgment-free zone 👇
